metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2 (or add a new AC2b) to assert that running `cursor-provenance.sh` twice against a temp `$HOME` also leaves exactly one `export AI_AGENT=cursor` line in `~/.profile` (mirroring the existing `~/.bashrc` idempotency check).
findings.finding: The What section asserts cursor-provenance.sh appends `export AI_AGENT=cursor` to BOTH `~/.bashrc` AND `~/.profile`, but AC2 only asserts idempotency for `~/.bashrc` ('leaves exactly one `export AI_AGENT=cursor` line in `~/.bashrc`'). The `~/.profile` modification is described work with no corresponding proof assertion in any AC.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, change 'leaves exactly one `export AI_AGENT=cursor` line in `~/.bashrc`' to 'leaves exactly one `export AI_AGENT=cursor` line in each of `~/.bashrc` and `~/.profile`'. In `test_harness_shims.py`, add assertions: `assert text_profile.count("export AI_AGENT=cursor") == 1` for the `~/.profile` path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2 to explicitly require the idempotency assertion for `~/.profile` (in addition to `~/.bashrc`), matching the What section's description that cursor-provenance.sh appends to both files.
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 proof command references `pytest tests/unit/test_harness_shims.py -k cursor -q`, but the file `tests/unit/test_harness_shims.py` does not yet exist; however this is expected for a creation plan. More critically, AC2 states the idempotency test checks '~/.bashrc' only, yet the What section explicitly says cursor-provenance.sh appends to BOTH `~/.bashrc` AND `~/.profile`. The AC as written does not enumerate the ~/.profile check as a required assertion, leaving the ~/.profile path unverified by the stated proof.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / What section (Cursor paragraph)
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC2 to read: '…leaves exactly one `export AI_AGENT=cursor` line in BOTH `~/.bashrc` AND `~/.profile` (idempotent, no accumulation in either file). Proof: `pytest tests/unit/test_harness_shims.py -k cursor -q`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2's idempotency proof to assert `~/.profile` also contains exactly one `export AI_AGENT=cursor` line after two runs (mirroring the existing `~/.bashrc` assertion).
findings.finding: AC2's idempotency proof covers only ~/.bashrc but the What section specifies cursor-provenance.sh appends to BOTH ~/.bashrc AND ~/.profile. The ~/.profile path has no corresponding idempotency AC assertion, leaving a behavioral gap: a second run could accumulate a duplicate 'export AI_AGENT=cursor' line in ~/.profile even while ~/.bashrc stays clean.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (`tests/unit/test_harness_shims.py -k cursor`); What section ('idempotently (guarded by grep -qF) appends … to the ephemeral … VM's shell profile — specifically ~/.bashrc and ~/.profile')
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_harness_shims.py`'s cursor idempotency test, add: `assert text_profile.count('export AI_AGENT=cursor') == 1` where `text_profile = (tmp_home / '.profile').read_text()`, parallel to the existing `~/.bashrc` count assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2 to also assert that running cursor-provenance.sh TWICE leaves exactly one `export AI_AGENT=cursor` line in `~/.profile` (mirroring the ~/.bashrc idempotency check), since the What section explicitly names both files as targets.
findings.finding: AC2 verifies idempotency only for ~/.bashrc but the What section explicitly describes the script appending to BOTH ~/.bashrc AND ~/.profile — the ~/.profile path is described work with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / What section (cursor-provenance.sh description)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC2 sentence: '…and in ~/.profile (idempotent in both files, no accumulation in either).' Update the test's k=cursor case to assert the same single-line invariant for ~/.profile.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document) the subprocess-sourcing path for `~/.bashrc`/`~/.profile` on the Cursor cloud-agent VM — if the agent runs tool commands in a non-login, non-interactive shell, the profile append won't propagate `AI_AGENT` to `rebar claim`; the plan should either confirm the Cursor VM sources these files for tool subprocesses, or pivot to a mechanism (e.g., a wrapper script that is itself the install target and explicitly sources the profile) that has a verified propagation path.
findings.finding: The Cursor `cursor-provenance.sh` appends `export AI_AGENT=cursor` to `~/.bashrc` and `~/.profile`, but neither file is guaranteed to be sourced when the Cursor cloud-agent invokes `rebar claim` as a non-interactive, non-login subprocess. `~/.bashrc` is only sourced by interactive bash shells; `~/.profile` only by login shells. The plan's data-flow assumption — that profile appending causes the env var to be present in the subcommand's environment — may silently fail, making the shim ineffective without any error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title (and any epic tracker entry) to reflect the actual Codex mechanism — e.g., 'Per-harness capture shims: Codex shell_environment_policy + Cursor install-script snippet' — so the title matches the design correction documented in the body.
findings.finding: The plan title names 'Codex SessionStart hook' as the Codex mechanism, but the body explicitly abandons this approach (hooks cannot export env vars) and uses `shell_environment_policy.set` instead. A developer reading only the title will expect a hook-based shim and may search for, or attempt to implement, the wrong mechanism.
findings.location: Plan title: 'Per-harness capture shims: Codex SessionStart hook + Cursor start-script snippet'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Per-harness capture shims: Codex shell_environment_policy + Cursor install-script snippet'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the capability assertion: confirm `shell_environment_policy.set = { AI_AGENT = "codex" }` is the correct key name and syntax in the Codex `~/.codex/config.toml` schema (e.g., add a code comment citing the specific vendor doc section/version, or quote the schema definition inline in the shim's comments) so reviewers can verify without fetching an external URL.
findings.finding: `shell_environment_policy.set = { AI_AGENT = "codex" }` is the entire load-bearing mechanism for the Codex shim, but this exact key name and syntax are not verifiable from any reachable file in the repo. The cited source (`developers.openai.com/codex/config-reference`) is an external URL that cannot be inspected; no test or existing file corroborates this key's existence or its effect of injecting env vars into every Codex subprocess.
findings.suggested_fix: In `codex-config.toml`, add a comment quoting the exact config-reference section title and version/date that documents `shell_environment_policy.set`, e.g.: `# Verified against: Codex CLI config reference §"Shell environment policy" (accessed <date>)\n# Key: shell_environment_policy.set — injects static key=value pairs into every subprocess env`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a stability caveat to the `experimental_use_profile` route in both the `codex-config.toml` comments and the `docs/session-id-shims.md` Codex section, noting that the `experimental_` prefix means this behaviour may change and users should verify against their installed Codex CLI version.
findings.finding: `experimental_use_profile = true` is presented as the documented route for users who want to set `REBAR_SESSION_ID` via their shell profile under Codex, but the `experimental_` prefix signals this key may be gated, unstable, or silently removed in future Codex versions. The plan proposes documenting this as a user-facing path in shim comments and docs without qualifying the stability risk.
findings.suggested_fix: In the shim comments and docs, add: `# NOTE: experimental_use_profile is prefixed 'experimental_' — verify it is supported in your Codex CLI version before relying on it.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the Cursor `environment.json` `install`-key capability assertion: add a comment in `cursor-environment.json` quoting the specific schema field name and the cursor.com docs section/version that documents it, so reviewers can verify the key is correct without fetching an external URL.
findings.finding: The Cursor `environment.json` `install` command mechanism is asserted as the correct injection point for the cloud-agent VM, citing `cursor.com/docs/cloud-agent/setup` and `environment.schema.json`, but neither source is reachable from the repo. No existing file or test in the repo corroborates that `.cursor/environment.json` supports an `install` key that runs a shell script on VM startup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact TOML key path for Codex static env injection (`shell_environment_policy.set` vs the actual schema) against the live Codex config-reference docs before finalising `codex-config.toml`, and record the verified key name + docs URL in the plan; if verification is not possible before implementation, add a SPIKE task.
findings.finding: `shell_environment_policy.set` is the sole Codex env-injection mechanism delivered, but the key name is unverified against the live Codex config-reference docs. No repo file contains this key or the cited URL (`developers.openai.com/codex/config-reference`). If the actual TOML key path differs (e.g. a different nesting, `shell_environment.set`, or a flat `[shell_environment_policy]` table header), Codex silently ignores the unknown key and `AI_AGENT` is never injected — the entire Codex harness-tag shim silently no-ops with no runtime error.
findings.suggested_fix: Before writing the file, confirm `~/.codex/config.toml` schema from https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-reference (or inspect an actual Codex-generated config). If the key cannot be verified pre-implementation, add a SPIKE to confirm, or add a runtime diagnostic comment in the shim noting how to verify injection is active (e.g. `codex run 'echo $AI_AGENT'`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a caveat in both `codex-config.toml` comments and the planned `docs/session-id-shims.md` Codex section that `experimental_use_profile` is an experimental Codex API that may not be present in all versions or enterprise deployments, and that users should verify it is active (e.g. by running a test subprocess) before relying on it.
findings.finding: `experimental_use_profile` is cited as 'a documented `shell_environment_policy` bool per the cited Codex config reference' and is planned to be documented in the shim comments as the route for `REBAR_SESSION_ID`. The `experimental_` prefix conventionally signals an unstable, potentially gated, or removable API surface. The plan does not caveat this instability, so users following the documented path may silently lose `REBAR_SESSION_ID` injection when Codex updates or in deployments where the experimental feature is disabled.
findings.location: What section — Codex paragraph: '`experimental_use_profile` route for `REBAR_SESSION_ID`'; `docs/session-id-shims.md` Codex section (planned)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `install` is a valid field in `.cursor/environment.json` against the live Cursor cloud-agent docs (`cursor.com/docs/cloud-agent/setup`) or the `environment.schema.json` before finalising `cursor-environment.json`, and record the confirmed field name in the plan.
findings.finding: The Cursor `environment.json` `install` field is asserted to be the mechanism by which `cursor-provenance.sh` is invoked, but the plan's own citation (`environment.schema.json`) is not reachable in this repo and no repo file confirms that `install` is a valid field name in `.cursor/environment.json`. The plan explicitly verified that an `env` field does NOT exist in this schema, but does not show equivalent verification that `install` DOES exist.
findings.location: What section — Cursor paragraph: '`cursor-environment.json` whose `install` command runs `cursor-provenance.sh`'; AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR (or a `docs/decisions/` entry or CLAUDE.md section) capturing the 'harness-tag-only; session-id via Secrets/profile' contract and the vendor-verified reason hooks/environment.json cannot export env vars, so future harness shim authors have a durable decision record.
findings.finding: The plan records a design correction — the epic's S6 premise was 'partly wrong' and a new architectural contract is established (harness-tag-only injection; session-id via manual Secrets/profile) — but this decision is routed only to a user-facing how-to doc (`docs/session-id-shims.md`), not an ADR or CLAUDE.md design-record entry. Future harness shim authors have no durable record of *why* session-id is handled this way, making the pattern easy to misrepresent or re-litigate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/decisions/adr-harness-session-id-limitation.md` (or a `## Design Decisions` section in CLAUDE.md) stating: (1) the S6 investigation finding, (2) the chosen contract (AI_AGENT injected via harness config; REBAR_SESSION_ID set manually), (3) the vendor-doc citations. Cross-reference it from `docs/session-id-shims.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum a `stderr` warning (e.g., `echo 'cursor-provenance.sh: failed to write ~/.bashrc, AI_AGENT not set' >&2`) before `exit 0` so failures are surfaced even when the exit code is suppressed.
findings.finding: cursor-provenance.sh uses `exit 0` to suppress all errors, meaning failures (e.g., permission denied on ~/.bashrc, missing $HOME, disk full) are silently swallowed with no log output. If the append fails, AI_AGENT is never set, but neither the Cursor install pipeline nor the user receives any signal — making the failure invisible and undiagnosable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace bare `exit 0` at the end of the error/catch path with: `echo "[cursor-provenance] WARNING: could not append to ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile; AI_AGENT may not be set" >&2; exit 0` — preserving fail-safe behavior while surfacing the failure to the install log.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
